cards.back: The main center holding the nucleus.
The thin, branch-like "antennas" receiving signals.
The single, long "tail" sending signals away.
The funnel-shaped base where the tail attaches to the body.
The insulating wrapper around the tail.
The bare gaps between the myelin wrappers.
The swollen tips at the very end of the tail.
Think Astro = Star. Look for star-shaped cells with many extensions.
Think Octopus. Reaches out with multiple branches to wrap several different axons in myelin.
Think Micro = Small. Noticeably smaller than other glia.
Packed tightly together like floor tiles, with tiny hairs (cilia) pointing into open fluid spaces.
Wraps around just one single axon segment, leaving a visible bulge on the edge where its nucleus sits.
Think Satellites orbiting. Small dot-like nuclei tightly circling around the perimeter of large sensory cell bodies.

sets.name: Four Major Regions of the Brain

cards.back: The large, wrinkly outer part of the brain. It handles your highest mental functions, like conscious perception, language, learning, and memory.
Located deep inside the brain. It contains the thalamus (routes incoming sensory information) and the hypothalamus (regulates internal balance and the autonomic nervous system).

The stalk connecting the brain to the spinal cord (midbrain, pons, and medulla). A vital pathway for nerve signals that automatically controls critical involuntary functions like heart rate and breathing.
Sitting at the back of the brain stem, it acts as a real-time motor comparator to coordinate your movements and keep you balanced.
The massive, highly wrinkled structure that makes up the top and sides of the brain, split into two halves by the longitudinal fissure.
A large, thick, C-shaped band of white matter deep inside the cerebrum that physically bridges the left and right hemispheres.
Buried deep beneath the cerebrum. Look for the thalamus (two egg-shaped masses touching in the middle) and the hypothalamus (funnels down into a sharp angle).
The cone-like structure connecting to the spinal cord. Spot the pons by looking for a thick bulge on the front, tapering down into the medulla.
Sitting at the lower back of the brain, it looks exactly like a miniature version of the cerebrum covered in tiny, parallel wrinkles.
8 pairs emerging from the neck.
12 pairs emerging from the upper back/chest region.
5 pairs emerging from the lower back.
5 pairs emerging from the sacrum (pelvic region).
1 single pair emerging from the coccyx (tailbone).
Reorganizes fibers from C1–C5.
Reorganizes fibers from C4–T1 to supply arm nerves.
Arises from the lumbar spinal nerves to supply the pelvic region and anterior leg.
Arises from the lower lumbar and sacral nerves to supply the posterior leg, forming the large sciatic nerve.
The tough, white outer layer that covers most of the eyeball.
The transparent front covering that protects the eye and lets light enter.
The colored smooth muscle of the eye that automatically opens and closes depending on light levels.
The central opening or hole within the iris where the light actually passes through.
The clear structure located just behind the pupil that bends and changes shape to focus incoming light.
The innermost lining of the eye made of nervous tissue that captures the visual image.
A tiny indentation at the exact center of the retina that provides your sharpest, clearest vision.

cards.front: Optic Disc (The Exit Door / Blind Spot)
cards.back: The spot at the back of the eye where the optic nerve exits to travel to the brain, creating a natural blind spot.
Light-sensitive cells in the retina for low-light conditions, functioning strictly in grayscale.
Cells in the retina that detect red, green, and blue wavelengths, providing sharp color vision.
A thin, watery fluid that fills the smaller anterior cavity in front of the lens.
A thick, viscous fluid that fills the large posterior cavity behind the lens.
Gray matter — the upper "wings" that process incoming sensory information.
Gray matter — the wider, lower "wings" that send out motor signals.
Gray matter — small bumps on the sides containing autonomic motor neurons.
The tiny "bullseye" hole right in the center of the gray matter containing cerebrospinal fluid.
White matter between the two upper wings carrying sensory info up to the brain.
White matter between the two lower wings carrying motor commands down from the brain.
White matter filling out the far left and right sides.
The deep, wide crack running right down the front midline.
The shallower, narrower groove running down the back midline.
Nerve fibers plugging into the back of the spinal cord, recognizable by the bulging dorsal root ganglion.
Nerve fibers emerging directly from the front of the spinal cord.
